title: Writing Your First Test
intro: You've decided to add tests to your task manager. You open the testing framework docs and stare at the blank test file. Let's walk through writing your first real test.

teach.title: Anatomy of a Test
teach.body: Every test has three phases: Arrange (set up the data), Act (run the function), and Assert (check the result). A test for adding a task would create input data, call the addTask function, and verify the returned task has the right properties.

teach.title: A Simple Unit Test
teach.annotation: The Arrange-Act-Assert pattern keeps tests readable and focused on one behavior.

teach.body: Start with the simplest test possible — one function, one input, one expected output. Build complexity gradually. A test that exists and runs is better than a perfect test you never write.

start.narrative: You're writing your first test for the task manager's 'completeTask' function. It should mark a task as done and set a completedAt timestamp. Where do you start?

choices.text: Write a test that checks the entire task lifecycle — create, complete, delete — all in one test
Write a focused test: given a pending task, completeTask should set status to 'done' and add a completedAt date
Skip writing tests and just manually click through the app to check it works

too-much.narrative: Your test is 40 lines long, creates a task, completes it, verifies it, then deletes it. When it fails, you can't tell which part broke. You split it into three separate tests, each checking one behavior.
too-much.feedback: One test per behavior is the golden rule. Mega-tests are hard to debug and maintain. You learned firsthand why focused tests matter.

focused-test.narrative: Your test passes. Now you want to test what happens when completeTask is called on an already-completed task. What do you do?

choices.text: Write a second test: 'should throw an error if task is already completed'
One passing test is enough, move on to building features

skip-tests.narrative: You manually test and it seems to work. Two weeks later, you refactor the task model and the completeTask function silently breaks — it no longer sets the timestamp. Users complain their completed tasks show no completion date.

choices.text: Fix the bug and finally write tests to prevent this from happening again
Fix the bug and go back to manual testing

edge-case.narrative: Your second test reveals that completeTask doesn't handle this edge case — it silently overwrites the completedAt timestamp. You add a guard clause and now have two solid tests covering the happy path and an edge case.
edge-case.feedback: This is exactly how testing should work. Focused tests that check specific behaviors, including edge cases. The test literally found a bug before your users did.

one-test-end.narrative: Your single test gives some confidence, but when the completeTask behavior changes, you don't catch edge cases. Users discover bugs you could have tested for.
one-test-end.feedback: One test is better than zero, but edge cases are where most bugs hide. Test the happy path and at least one error case for each function.

late-test-end.narrative: You write regression tests for the bug. Future refactors are now safer, though users already experienced the bug.
late-test-end.feedback: Regression tests are valuable — they prevent the same bug from recurring. But writing tests upfront would have caught this before users were affected.

manual-end.narrative: The same pattern repeats: you fix a bug, it comes back later after a refactor, you fix it again. Each cycle costs more time than writing a test would have.
manual-end.feedback: Without automated tests, bugs keep recurring. The time spent fixing recurring bugs always exceeds the time it takes to write tests.
